content.text: What Is a CanNotDelete Lock?

A CanNotDelete lock prevents any user or service principal from deleting the locked resource or its children, while still allowing all read and write operations. It is the most commonly used lock type for production resources because it does not interfere with normal deployment and configuration workflows — teams can still update resources, deploy new app versions, scale, and configure — but cannot accidentally (or intentionally) delete infrastructure.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing CanNotDelete lock scope inheritance, allowed write operations, and blocked delete operations

content.text: CanNotDelete is the standard production lock. It blocks only the DELETE HTTP verb at the ARM layer. All GET, PUT, PATCH, and POST operations continue to work normally.
Applying and Removing a CanNotDelete Lock

Locks are created with az lock create specifying --lock-type CanNotDelete. The scope can be a resource group (most common), a subscription, or an individual resource. Removing a lock requires Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete permission — included in Owner and User Access Administrator roles, but NOT in Contributor. This intentional permission gap prevents a Contributor from bypassing delete protection.

# Apply a CanNotDelete lock to a production resource group
az lock create \
  --name 'prod-cannotdelete' \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --notes 'Prevents accidental deletion of production resources'

# Apply to a specific critical resource (a storage account holding audit logs)
az lock create \
  --name 'audit-logs-nodelete' \
  --resource-group rg-security \
  --resource-name sa-audit-logs \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --notes 'Audit log retention compliance requirement'

# Apply at subscription scope — all resource groups in the subscription are protected
az lock create \
  --name 'sub-nodelete' \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv) \
  --notes 'Subscription-wide delete protection'

# List all CanNotDelete locks in a resource group
az lock list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[?properties.level=='CanNotDelete'].{Name:name,Level:properties.level,Notes:properties.notes}" \
  -o table

# Remove a CanNotDelete lock (requires Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete)
az lock delete \
  --name 'prod-cannotdelete' \
  --resource-group rg-prod
Verifying CanNotDelete Behavior

The error message when a delete is blocked is 409 Conflict with error code ScopeLocked or RequestConflict. Writes — including tag updates, VM restarts, configuration changes — succeed normally. Run the following sequence to verify lock behavior before relying on it in production.

# Test CanNotDelete behavior

# 1. Apply the lock
az lock create --name 'test-nodelete' --resource-group rg-test --lock-type CanNotDelete

# 2. Verify writes still work (update a tag)
az tag update \
  --resource-id $(az group show --name rg-test --query id -o tsv) \
  --operation merge \
  --tags Environment=test
# Expected: success — writes are allowed

# 3. Attempt to delete a resource — should be blocked
az storage account delete \
  --name sa-test-01 \
  --resource-group rg-test \
  --yes
# Expected: (RequestConflict) The scope '/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/rg-test' is locked

# 4. Attempt to delete the resource group itself — should be blocked
az group delete --name rg-test --yes
# Expected: (ScopeLocked) The scope '/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/rg-test' is locked

# 5. Confirm VM restart still works (write operation, not delete)
az vm restart --resource-group rg-test --name vm-test-01
# Expected: success — restart is a POST/PATCH, not a DELETE

# 6. Remove lock and retry deletion
az lock delete --name 'test-nodelete' --resource-group rg-test
az storage account delete --name sa-test-01 --resource-group rg-test --yes
# Expected: success after lock is removed
Common Lock-Blocked Scenarios and Workarounds

CanNotDelete locks can surface in unexpected places because delete operations are more common in infrastructure management than they appear. Several ARM operations that feel like configuration changes actually submit a DELETE under the covers, and these will be blocked.
Scenario
Why Blocked?
Workaround
terraform destroy fails on locked resources
Terraform DELETEs resources in dependency order; lock stops it
Remove lock resource in Terraform first, then destroy; or use -target to remove lock before destroy
Removing a subnet from a VNet
DELETE on the subnet sub-resource
Remove lock, delete subnet, re-apply lock
Deleting a diagnostic settings entry
DELETE on the diagnostic settings resource
Remove lock, delete settings, re-apply lock
Removing an NSG rule via portal
Portal submits DELETE on the rule sub-resource
Remove lock at NSG scope, update NSG, re-apply lock
az group delete --force in a CI/CD pipeline
DELETE on the RG; lock propagates down from subscription
Lock should be on individual resources, not subscription, if pipelines need to delete RGs
Moving a resource to another subscription
Move operation requires delete from source scope
Remove lock before move; re-apply after
Terraform: Locks as Code

Defining locks as Terraform resources alongside the protected resources ensures that the lock is always present and that changes are reviewed in pull requests. When a destroy is needed, the lock resource must be destroyed first — Terraform handles the dependency ordering automatically because the lock's scope references the parent resource.

# terraform/production-locks.tf
# Apply CanNotDelete locks to all production resources via Terraform

resource "azurerm_resource_group" "prod" {
  name     = "rg-prod"
  location = "eastus"
  tags     = { Environment = "prod" }
}

# CanNotDelete lock on the resource group
resource "azurerm_management_lock" "rg_prod" {
  name       = "prod-cannotdelete"
  scope      = azurerm_resource_group.prod.id
  lock_level = "CanNotDelete"
  notes      = "Protects production resource group from accidental deletion"
}

# CanNotDelete lock on a critical storage account
resource "azurerm_storage_account" "prod_data" {
  name                     = "saproddata01"
  resource_group_name      = azurerm_resource_group.prod.name
  location                 = azurerm_resource_group.prod.location
  account_tier             = "Standard"
  account_replication_type = "GRS"
}

resource "azurerm_management_lock" "sa_prod_data" {
  name       = "sa-prod-data-nodelete"
  scope      = azurerm_storage_account.prod_data.id
  lock_level = "CanNotDelete"
  notes      = "Retention compliance — storage account must not be deleted before data expiry"
}

# Lock on a VNet to prevent accidental deletion of networking
resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "prod" {
  name                = "vnet-prod"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.prod.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.prod.location
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
}

resource "azurerm_management_lock" "vnet_prod" {
  name       = "vnet-prod-nodelete"
  scope      = azurerm_virtual_network.prod.id
  lock_level = "CanNotDelete"
  notes      = "VNet deletion would disconnect all production resources"
}

# To destroy locked resources:
# terraform destroy -target=azurerm_management_lock.rg_prod
# terraform destroy -target=azurerm_management_lock.sa_prod_data
# terraform destroy -target=azurerm_management_lock.vnet_prod
# terraform destroy
Enforcing Lock Presence with Azure Policy

A CanNotDelete lock applied manually can be removed by an Owner. To ensure production resource groups always have a lock, use a DeployIfNotExists policy that deploys the lock automatically when a production resource group exists without one. The policy's managed identity needs Owner role to create Microsoft.Authorization/locks resources.
CanNotDelete vs Delete Lock in ARM Templates

ARM templates and Bicep can create locks as nested resources within the resource being protected. This is the most atomic approach — the lock is deployed in the same template as the resource, ensuring the two are never separated.
Bulk-Applying Locks to All Production Resource Groups

Manually applying a CanNotDelete lock to each production resource group is error-prone and does not scale. The pattern below uses the Azure CLI to iterate over all resource groups tagged Environment=prod and apply the lock idempotently, skipping resource groups that are already locked. Run this script as an Azure Automation Runbook on a schedule to catch new resource groups that were created after the initial sweep.

# Apply CanNotDelete locks to all production resource groups in a subscription
# This script is idempotent — it skips RGs that already have the lock

LOCK_NAME='prod-cannotdelete'
LOCK_NOTES='Production resource group — deletion protection'

# Get all resource groups tagged Environment=prod
RGS=$(az group list --query "[?tags.Environment=='prod'].name" -o tsv)

for RG in $RGS; do
  # Check if lock already exists
  EXISTING=$(az lock list --resource-group $RG \
    --query "[?name=='$LOCK_NAME'] | length(@)" -o tsv)

  if [[ $EXISTING -eq 0 ]]; then
    az lock create \
      --name $LOCK_NAME \
      --resource-group $RG \
      --lock-type CanNotDelete \
      --notes "$LOCK_NOTES"
    echo "Lock applied: $RG"
  else
    echo "Already locked: $RG"
  fi
done

echo '=== Final lock inventory ==='
for RG in $RGS; do
  echo "Resource group: $RG"
  az lock list --resource-group $RG \
    --query '[].{Name:name,Level:properties.level}' -o table
done
Auditing Lock Coverage with Resource Graph

Before relying on CanNotDelete locks for compliance, verify that all production resource groups actually have a lock applied. Resource Graph provides the fastest way to check lock coverage across an entire management group in a single query. Use these queries for weekly compliance reports and to drive remediation.

# Audit: find prod resource groups without a CanNotDelete lock

# Using Resource Graph (fastest — single API call across management group)
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| where tags['Environment'] =~ 'prod'
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
    | where properties.level =~ 'CanNotDelete'
    | project lockRg = resourceGroup
) on \$left.name == \$right.lockRg
| where isnull(lockRg)
| project rgName = name, subscriptionId, environment = tags['Environment']
| order by subscriptionId asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Count locks by type across management group
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
| summarize Count = count() by LockLevel = tostring(properties.level), subscriptionId
| order by subscriptionId asc, LockLevel asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Find resources that have a lock but whose parent RG is unprotected
# (locks on individual resources without a RG-level lock are incomplete protection)
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
| where properties.level =~ 'CanNotDelete'
| where resourceGroup != ''
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
    | where properties.level =~ 'CanNotDelete'
    | project rgLockGroup = resourceGroup
    | where isnull(rgLockGroup)  # locks on the RG itself have empty resourceGroup
) on \$left.resourceGroup == \$right.rgLockGroup
| project lockedResource = id, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table
Emergency Lock Removal and Re-Application Pattern

When an incident or emergency maintenance requires deleting a resource that is protected by a CanNotDelete lock, follow a structured remove-act-restore pattern. The critical principle is that the lock MUST be re-applied even if the emergency action fails. Use a shell trap or an always-condition step in a CI/CD pipeline to guarantee the lock is restored.

# Incident response: safely remove a lock, perform emergency action, restore
# This pattern ensures the lock is always re-applied even if the emergency action fails

RESOURCE_GROUP='rg-prod'
LOCK_NAME='prod-cannotdelete'
LOCK_TYPE='CanNotDelete'
LOCK_NOTES='Production resource group — deletion protection'

# Step 1: Capture lock details before removing
LOCK_EXISTS=$(az lock list --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
  --query "[?name=='$LOCK_NAME'] | length(@)" -o tsv)

if [[ $LOCK_EXISTS -gt 0 ]]; then
  echo 'Removing lock temporarily for emergency operation'
  az lock delete --name $LOCK_NAME --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP
fi

# Step 2: Perform the emergency action (example: delete a stuck resource)
EMERGENCY_FAILED=0
az resource delete \
  --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
  --name vm-stuck-01 \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines || EMERGENCY_FAILED=1

# Step 3: ALWAYS re-apply the lock (use || true so script continues even if lock create fails)
if [[ $LOCK_EXISTS -gt 0 ]]; then
  az lock create \
    --name $LOCK_NAME \
    --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
    --lock-type $LOCK_TYPE \
    --notes "$LOCK_NOTES"
  echo 'Lock re-applied'
fi

if [[ $EMERGENCY_FAILED -eq 1 ]]; then
  echo 'Emergency action failed — lock re-applied regardless; investigate manually'
  exit 1
fi

echo 'Emergency operation complete — lock status:'
az lock list --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP -o table
PowerShell: Bulk Lock Management Across Subscriptions

For environments spanning multiple subscriptions, the Azure PowerShell module enables iterating over management group subscriptions and applying locks in bulk. This is particularly useful as a scheduled Automation Runbook that enforces lock compliance continuously.
Lock Limits and Design Considerations
Azure imposes resource limits on locks that affect design decisions at scale.
Limit
Value
Implication
Maximum locks per scope
Apply at RG level, not individual resources, to stay within limits at scale
Lock inheritance depth
All child resources
One RG-level lock protects all hundreds of resources within it
Lock removal permission
Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete
Owner and User Access Administrator only — plan RBAC accordingly
Lock scope minimum
Individual resource
Can lock a single disk without locking its parent VM or RG
Cross-subscription move with lock
Blocked
Must remove lock before moving a resource to a different subscription
ARM template replacement behavior
Replacement blocked
If ARM replaces a resource (delete+create), the delete step is blocked — use patch-in-place instead

Apply CanNotDelete locks at the resource group level, not at individual resources. One RG lock protects everything inside it, stays well within the 20-lock limit, and is easier to audit than locks scattered across individual resources.
Summary

A CanNotDelete lock is the standard protection for production Azure resources. It blocks all DELETE operations while allowing reads, writes, and all data-plane access. Apply it at resource group scope to protect all resources within the group. Removing a lock requires Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete (Owner or User Access Administrator — not Contributor). Use Terraform to manage locks as code alongside the resources they protect, and use a DeployIfNotExists policy to automatically restore locks that are removed. The lock does not protect against all destructive operations — Modify and Replace operations (which delete and re-create a resource internally) may still run; test your specific resource types to confirm behavior.
